Interesting idea. Issues:

1. The DiscoServer does not purge its registrations when the stream is killed. I also think that we should not change that.
2. Users of this service will be slightly surprised to see their registrations gone when PEP support disappears after a reconnect.

The solution would be to provide a callback users of the PEPService can register to to get notified if PEP support is gone and their registrations are dead. I do not like that API though, and I’m open for better solutions.

Cool. When I was writing this I realized that our current implementation is a bit limited - it doesn't allow generating and logging _only_ a client span. I.e. the outermost `zipkin_span` contextmanager is always going to report ('sr', 'ss').

This is _probably_ okay, since you'll only really ever log client spans in the context of another span. But in this context it means that we don't need to check for 'sr', because the outermost `zipkin_span` (i.e. the one with the logging_context) is always going to log it.

Having this only on the last epoch seems counter to the purpose of printing out a validation WER. It should be configurable or maybe happen all the time but have `is_validation_step` set to a computationally reasonable value.

Traditionally one prints out a validation WER to monitor the generalization of the model as it trains. To do this one needs to see the training WER and the validation WER throughout training to see if the training WER dips significantly below the validation WER.

When this happens you want to stop training, as this is a signal of over fitting and subsequent epochs will only server to further overfit and thus waste GPU time.

`import aiohttp` theoretically may raise `ImportError` when one of its dependencies is not installed. 

`from .auth import JWTAuth` and other imports below it may raise ImportError due to a bug, or when some dependencies are not installed as well.

So, I would do this:

```
    . . .
except ImportError as err:
    warnings.warn(str(err))
```

I'm using this in a script I'm working on and I noticed that the `service_name` is actually the endpoint prefix.  What do you think about getting this updated to use botocore's service name?

The code I had was:

```
for service_name in session.get_available_services():
    for region_name in session.get_available_regions(service_name):
        # ...
```

Given other session methods that take service name use botocore's service names, it seems like `get_available_regions` should probably also use the same names:

```
>>> s = botocore.session.get_session()
>>> s.create_client('elb')
<botocore.client.ElasticLoadBalancing object at 0x10f82df10>
>>> s.get_service_model('elb')
<botocore.model.ServiceModel object at 0x10f780e10>
>>> s.get_available_regions('elb')
[]
>>> s.get_available_regions('elasticloadbalancing')
[u'us-east-1', u'us-west-1', u'us-west-2', ...]
```

if we use `id(obj)` we avoid any kind of reference counting for the GC. This introduces a little overhead:
```
In [53]: timeit.timeit("id({'a':5})", number=10000)
Out[53]: 0.0017991065979003906

In [34]: timeit.timeit("{'a':5}", number=10000)
Out[34]: 0.0015370845794677734
```

that I think it's not relevant. The only caveat is that we have these results for CPython, and it could be different for other interpreters, but at the moment we officially support only CPython.

Also `_get_chunks` should return a tuple of tuples. One tuple for each dimension listing the size of each chunk in that dimension. So:

```python
>>> _get_chunks((4, 4), 4)
((2, 2), (2, 2))
>>> _get_chunks((4, 4), 4)
((2, 2), (4,))
>>> _get_chunks((5, 5), 2)
((2, 3), (5,))
>>> _get_chunks((2, 4), 2)
((1, 1), (4,))  # ? or it might be better to have ((2,), (2, 2))
```

> For example, I still don't fully grasp the meaning of the CYC signal. It seems pretty useless to me (except maybe for some interconnects).

As you suggested, I use it in interconnects in order to keep a channel from a master to a slave 'alive'. It is used to request the arbiter for a channel. Once access is granted (GNT), the master can perform any number of writes/reads (even mixed), or wait between operations, and the interconnect ensures that the channel is not 'deallocated' until the last ACK is received.

However, I think that it is not clear enough what a 'cycle' is in the spec.

> https://cdn.opencores.org/downloads/wbspec_b4.pdf Page 29:
> The write enable output [WE_O] indicates whether the current local bus cycle is a READ
or WRITE cycle.  The signal is negated during READ cycles, and is asserted during WRITE
cycles.

From the quote above, and the description of '[CYC_O]', it seems that changing WE is not allowed. But, in pages 50 and 52, WE is (de)asserted explicitly once for each 'continuous' block transfer. See 'Phase (Bus Cycle)' in page 20.

I added the try/except inside the loop to fix a problem that was reported by a user (in issue #209). Basically, evaluating the expression `bdb.transactions.status(txid).get('status')` can raise an exception before it can be compared with `'valid'`. I resolved that issue in pull request #244.

It does, in my testing the difference between valid CEK and invalid CEKis still measurable in a tight lopp on the local machine, but hardly over the network where the rest of the stack noise is greater than the timing difference. raising an exception there lowered the difference between the code path of about .4% on average in my testing, so I added it. It is not perfect, but can't be,

Note that I measured up to 3% variability in decoding 2 identical messages encrypted with difference CEKs, so as long as we stay in the error margin the attacker has too much noise to figure out what he got.
